---
title: "R Markdown 1 — Document Anatomy"
subtitle: "YAML, Markdown prose, and clean-session knitting"
author: "R. Burke Squires"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: false
    code_folding: show
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Goal for this 20-minute block

By the end of this block, you will be able to identify the three parts of an `.Rmd` file, edit YAML and Markdown safely, and knit the complete learner report.

> **Work only in `student_work/morning_report.Rmd`.** This lesson page is read-only course material.

# The three-part anatomy

Every R Markdown source combines:

1. **YAML** — metadata and output settings between `---` lines at the top.
2. **Markdown prose** — headings, paragraphs, emphasis, lists, links, and other narrative structure.
3. **Code chunks and inline code** — calculations whose results are inserted when the document knits.

# YAML

The learner file begins with:

```` yaml
---
title: "My Morning R Markdown Report"
author: "Your name"
date: "`r knitr::inline_expr('Sys.Date()')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    code_folding: show
---
````

Useful fields include:

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `title` | Document title | `"Biomarker Summary"` |
| `author` | Author or team | `"J. Smith"` |
| `date` | Fixed or computed date | `` "`r knitr::inline_expr('Sys.Date()')`" `` |
| `output` | Output format and options | `html_document` |

YAML indentation uses spaces. Do not use tabs.

# Markdown prose

| Source | Result |
|---|---|
| `**important**` | **important** |
| `*emphasis*` | *emphasis* |
| `` `object_name` `` | `object_name` |
| `## Methods` | A second-level heading |
| `- item` | A bullet item |
| `[NIEHS](https://www.niehs.nih.gov)` | A link |

Use headings to express the document's logical structure, not merely to make text larger.

# What knitting does

When you click **Knit**, R Markdown:

1. Starts a fresh R session.
2. Executes chunks in document order through **knitr**.
3. Inserts results into an intermediate Markdown document.
4. Uses **Pandoc** to create the requested output.

That fresh session is essential. Objects left in the Console or Global Environment are not dependable inputs to the report. Every package, data file, and object needed by the analysis must be created or loaded by the source document.

::: {.callout-note}
A successful knit is strong evidence that the document contains its computational dependencies. It does not by itself prove that the scientific methods or conclusions are correct; those still require review.
:::

# Hands-on — edit the learner report

Open `student_work/morning_report.Rmd` and complete these steps:

1. Change the author to your name.
2. Replace the title with a report title relevant to your work.
3. In **Purpose**, add one **bold** phrase and one *italic* phrase.
4. Replace the two placeholder bullets under **Notes** with two learning goals.
5. Click **Knit**.
6. Close the rendered preview, restart R, and knit again.

## Success criterion

The report knits after an R restart, and your title, author, formatting, and learning goals appear in the output.

# Troubleshooting pattern

When knitting fails:

1. Read the first useful error message.
2. Note the file and chunk or line named in the message.
3. Fix that first error rather than changing several unrelated things.
4. Knit again from the top.
5. Use `student_work/checkpoints/morning_report_complete.Rmd` only if you need a recovery copy.

# What's next

Keep `student_work/morning_report.Rmd` open. The next lesson adds chunk controls and inline results.
